blueprint_refs: poi_topk_recall: "recall@K of ground-truth POIs appearing in the simulated day (set-based), average across days. K is fixed (e.g., 5 or length of simulated sequence, designed)"

evidence.metrics: topk_poi_recall mean = 0.0 on validation; also 0.0 for every calibration iter

poi-recall-always-zero-set-vs-topk-bug.error_identification: POI recall is exactly 0.0 across all calibration iterations and the held-out evaluation, indicating the metric implementation is likely wrong (or always comparing disjoint sets).
poi-recall-always-zero-set-vs-topk-bug.root_cause_analysis: In Evaluator.compute_metrics(), recall is computed as intersection(gt_set, sim_pois_k)/len(gt_set). This is not recall@K as defined: denominator should be |GT| but numerator should compare to a simulated *set* of size K (or all simulated if shorter). Additionally, using the first K simulated visits (ordered) plus dividing by full GT set can drive values toward 0 when GT sizes are typically >K; the blueprint allows K to be 5 or length of simulated sequence, but denominator must be min(K, |GT|) if using recall@K or remain |GT| if using coverage recall but then K should not truncate the simulated set. Current hybrid makes the metric overly harsh and can appear degenerate when sequences are longer than K.
poi-recall-always-zero-set-vs-topk-bug.correct_approach:
Implement recall@K correctly and deterministically:
- Let sim_set = set(sim_pois[:K]).
- Let gt_set = set(gt_pois).
- Define recall_at_k = |gt_set ∩ sim_set| / min(len(gt_set), K) (standard recall@K), OR if you want "coverage of GT by top-K simulated" then keep denominator len(gt_set) but do not call it recall@K and expect small values.
Also handle cases where sim has <K by using min(K, len(sim)).
poi-recall-always-zero-set-vs-topk-bug.key_insight: When a metric is used as a calibration signal, implement it exactly per definition; a subtly mis-specified denominator/truncation can collapse the signal (here: always near 0), making calibration chase the wrong objective.

blueprint_refs: TrajectoryStringFormatter.FORMAT_SPEC: "comma+space separators and ends with a single '.'"

evidence.metrics: Sample outputs show '; ' separators and no trailing '.' (e.g., "... at 22:11:18" without period).

trajectory-string-format-mismatch-comma-.error_identification: Serialized trajectories do not follow the declared format_spec: code emits semicolon-separated visits and omits the required trailing period, while format_spec claims comma+space and trailing '.'. This can break downstream parsers/scorers and invalidates output contract consistency.
trajectory-string-format-mismatch-comma-.root_cause_analysis: TrajectoryStringFormatter documents a canonical comma-separated, period-terminated format, but format_day_string() returns f"{prefix} " + "; ".join(parts) and never appends a period. The parser parse_1921y_records() also expects semicolons, creating an internal inconsistency with FORMAT_SPEC.
trajectory-string-format-mismatch-comma-.correct_approach:
Make formatter and parser consistent with the canonical spec:
- Update format_day_string() to use ", " separators and append ".".
- Update parse_1921y_records() splitting logic to accept commas (and optionally semicolons for robustness) but standardize emission.
- Ensure TrajectoryStringFormatter.FORMAT_SPEC matches actual emission.
trajectory-string-format-mismatch-comma-.key_insight: Never let the declared format_spec diverge from emitted strings; keep a single canonical serializer and have parsers accept supersets for robustness.

blueprint_refs: simulation_evaluation.metrics: stop_count_mae, category_mix_jsd, time_of_day_emd, poi_topk_recall, radius_of_gyration_error

evidence.metrics: Reported metrics are category_share_mae, tod_jsd_avg, trip_distance_wasserstein, transition_divergence, stop_count_kl, etc., which do not match blueprint names/definitions.

evaluation-metrics-not-matching-blueprin.error_identification: The simulator is evaluated and calibrated on a different metric suite than required by the blueprint (e.g., uses JSD over time bins instead of time_of_day_emd; uses share MAE instead of category_mix_jsd; missing radius_of_gyration_error). This makes reported performance incomparable to spec and can mis-tune parameters.
evaluation-metrics-not-matching-blueprin.root_cause_analysis: Evaluator.compute_metrics() implements category_share_mae and tod_jsd_avg and trip_distance_wasserstein, but blueprint requires category_mix_jsd and time_of_day_emd and radius_of_gyration_error. There is no implementation of radius of gyration; time metric is JSD not EMD; category metric is MAE not JSD. Calibration objective weights also target these non-blueprint metrics.
evaluation-metrics-not-matching-blueprin.correct_approach:
Align evaluation to blueprint:
- Replace category_share_mae with category_mix_jsd using js_divergence() over (fine or super) category distribution.
- Replace tod_jsd_avg with time_of_day_emd by computing minutes-of-day samples or histograms and using wasserstein_1d() as an EMD proxy over binned distributions.
- Implement radius_of_gyration_error: compute daily radius of gyration for simulated and GT using POI lat/lon; exclude visits with missing coords (as blueprint allows) and aggregate absolute/relative error.
- Keep extra diagnostics metrics if desired, but report and optimize the blueprint-required ones.
evaluation-metrics-not-matching-blueprin.key_insight: Calibration optimizes exactly what you measure; if your metric suite drifts from spec, you’ll tune the wrong behavior even if the simulator ‘looks good’ on internal metrics.

evidence.metrics: transition_divergence ~= 0.6931471803 (≈ ln(2)) consistently across calibration and evaluation

transition-divergence-stuck-at-ln2-indic.error_identification: Transition divergence is pegged at ~ln(2), a common maximum for JSD between two highly distinct distributions, suggesting simulated vs GT transition bigram distributions share little overlap or the computation is collapsing to a two-point support.
transition-divergence-stuck-at-ln2-indic.root_cause_analysis: Two likely contributing mechanisms in code: (1) The simulator generates super-category sequences (sc_seq) and then selects POIs by supercat; if GT transitions are computed over fine categories or have different supercat mapping, overlap can be low. (2) In Evaluator.compute_metrics(), if either gt_bigram or sim_bigram is empty it falls back to {"None":1.0}; but here value is ln(2) not 0, implying both are non-empty but possibly dominated by disjoint keys due to inconsistent category->supercat mapping or because simulation uses a restricted tuned_supercats subset while GT uses full supercat space.
transition-divergence-stuck-at-ln2-indic.correct_approach:
Disambiguate then fix:
- Add diagnostics: number of unique bigrams in GT vs SIM; top-20 bigrams and overlaps.
- Ensure both GT and SIM transitions are computed on the same taxonomy (either fine categories or super-categories per catto.json).
- Avoid restricting the transition support to tuned_supercats during simulation; keep full supercat support from dataset.poi_tokens_by_supercat keys + 'Unknown' so simulated bigrams can match GT support.
- Consider adding per-user smoothing rather than hard global fallback to reduce mode collapse.
transition-divergence-stuck-at-ln2-indic.key_insight: When a divergence metric saturates at a constant across parameter sweeps, treat it as a taxonomy/support mismatch or a collapsed feature extractor; add overlap diagnostics and ensure identical representation for GT vs SIM.

blueprint_refs: radius_of_gyration_error: "requires POI lat/lon"; "handle missing POIs by excluding those visits"
radius-of-gyration-not-computable-becaus.error_identification: Ground-truth step distances are never computed (gt_total_steps=0) on the evaluated split, making any distance-based comparison unreliable and indicating GT POIs may not join to coordinates or GT sequences are too short after parsing.
radius-of-gyration-not-computable-becaus.root_cause_analysis: Evaluator.add_step_dist() increments gt_total_steps for i in range(1,len(gt)), so gt_total_steps=0 implies almost all GT days have <=1 visit after parsing, or POI tokens in GT do not match dataset.poi_by_token keys (so visits exist but steps not counted would still increment totals—however totals increment before coord check, so this points to len(gt)<=1). That suggests parse_1921y_records() may be splitting incorrectly for the dataset delimiter (expects ';'), causing it to treat multi-visit strings as a single token or fail to parse additional visits.
radius-of-gyration-not-computable-becaus.correct_approach:
Make parsing robust to the true delimiter(s) in 1921Y.json:
- Inspect raw day strings to confirm whether visits are separated by ';' or ','.
- Update parse_1921y_records() to split on both ';' and ',' (careful to not split inside categories/POI names) or use a regex to extract repeated "<loc> at <time>" patterns.
- After fix, assert per-day visit counts distribution for GT has reasonable mean >1 and gt_total_steps>0 in diagnostics.
Also implement radius_of_gyration_error per blueprint once coordinates join is working.
radius-of-gyration-not-computable-becaus.key_insight: If GT-derived trajectory stats (like step counts) are zero, debug parsing/joining before tuning the generator; otherwise all spatial metrics become meaningless.

evidence.metrics: SIMULATION RESULTS show calibrated_parameters.best_objective_on_training/best_params, while code writes best_objective/best_parameters in calibrated_parameters.json

output-contract-mismatch-calibrated-para.error_identification: The on-disk output structure appears inconsistent with what the run summary expects (results loader reports best_objective_on_training/best_params, but code writes best_objective and best_parameters). This risks downstream tooling failing to read results consistently.
output-contract-mismatch-calibrated-para.root_cause_analysis: In main(), calibrated_parameters dict uses keys "best_parameters" and "best_objective". However, SIMULATION RESULTS.calibrated_parameters shows different keys ("best_params", "best_objective_on_training"), implying an external evaluator/loader expects those names or the saved file differs from the code’s intent.
output-contract-mismatch-calibrated-para.correct_approach:
Stabilize output schema by matching the actually consumed keys:
- In calibrated_parameters.json, include both naming variants (aliases) to be safe: write best_params and best_parameters pointing to same object; best_objective_on_training and best_objective same value.
- Keep a single canonical set in future, but don’t break existing loaders.
output-contract-mismatch-calibrated-para.key_insight: When outputs are consumed by external evaluators, treat key names as API contracts; add backward-compatible aliases rather than renaming fields outright.
